none: Environmental Impact Statement, Land and Water Conservation Fund Project

The Illinois Department of Conservation plans to acquire 725 acres along Lake Michigan's shoreline between Zion and the Illinois-Wisconsin border. This land will extend Illinois Beach State Park for recreation and conservation, reversing residential development trends. While it will displace 240 homeowners and temporarily reduce local tax revenue, it aims to preserve open space and provide recreational opportunities for the Chicago and Milwaukee areas. The project will also address shoreline erosion and protect delicate ecosystems, with development expected to start in 1974-75.
Environmental Impact Statement, Land and Water Conservation Fund Project

The Illinois Department of Conservation plans to acquire 725 acres along Lake Michigan's shoreline between Zion and the Illinois-Wisconsin border. This land will extend Illinois Beach State Park for recreation and conservation, reversing residential development trends. While it will displace 240 homeowners and temporarily reduce local tax revenue, it aims to preserve open space and provide recreational opportunities for the Chicago and Milwaukee areas. The project will also address shoreline erosion and protect delicate ecosystems, with development expected to start in 1974-75.

The project was proposed to combat the negative impacts of residential development along Lake Michigan's shoreline and to enhance conservation efforts in the area. While there was public pushback due to the displacement of homeowners and concerns over reduced local tax revenue, the initiative aimed to create a balance between recreation and environmental preservation. Ultimately, the project was implemented to protect the shoreline and promote recreational opportunities.

"We do have citizens who feel that is an imposition on our citizenry to bring people from the metropolitan area of Milwaukee and Chicago to congest this area." — Letter of July 19, 1972, from the Mayor of the City of Zion

"This is one of the few areas that is still available with Lake Michigan frontage. I think this is great foresight by the Governor to take this progressive action at this time while the property is available at not too great a cost." — Letter of July 19, 1972, from the Mayor of the City of Zion
